stem: The vector field \( \mathbf{F}(x,y) = \left( ([[a]])y + ([[b]]),\; ([[a]])x + ([[c]]) \right) \) is conservative. Evaluate the closed-loop integral \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \), where \( C \) is the circle \( x^{2} + y^{2} = [[R2]] \) traversed once counterclockwise.
solution: A conservative field is the gradient of a potential \( \varphi \) (here \( \varphi = ([[a]])xy + ([[b]])x + ([[c]])y \)). The line integral over any closed curve returns to its starting potential value, so \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = 0 \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes a nonzero result proportional to the enclosed area \( \pi R^{2} \) mishandled as \( a R^{2} \); a conservative field has zero circulation.
This doubles a spurious area term; the true circulation of a conservative field is 0.
This adds the constant components of the field, which do not contribute to a closed-loop integral of a gradient.
This is a small nonzero value; the correct closed-loop integral of a conservative field is exactly 0.
This is a small nonzero value; the correct closed-loop integral of a conservative field is exactly 0.
This is a small nonzero value; the correct closed-loop integral of a conservative field is exactly 0.
This is a small nonzero value; the correct closed-loop integral of a conservative field is exactly 0.

stem: The vector field \( \mathbf{F}(x,y) = \left( ([[a]])y + ([[b]])x^{[[n]]},\; ([[a]])x \right) \) is conservative for every choice of exponent \( [[n]] \), since \( \partial_y P = \partial_x Q = ([[a]]) \) regardless of the \( x^{[[n]]} \) term. Let \( C \) be the square with corners \( (\pm [[s]], \pm [[s]]) \), traversed once counterclockwise. Evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
solution: A potential is \( \varphi(x,y) = ([[a]])xy + \tfrac{([[b]])}{[[n]]+1}x^{[[n]]+1} \), since \( \nabla\varphi=\mathbf{F} \) (the \( x^{[[n]]} \) term never breaks conservativeness because it only appears in \( P \) and does not depend on \( y \)). Any closed loop integral of a conservative field is 0, regardless of the loop's shape, so \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = 0 \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the nonlinear term as if it contributed a nonzero circulation; it does not, since the field is still conservative.
This assumes the field behaves like a pure rotation field of magnitude \( a \) around the square; a conservative field's closed-loop integral is 0 regardless.
This guesses a value proportional to the square's perimeter/area; it is not how a conservative field's circulation works (it is always 0).
This is a small nonzero value; the correct closed-loop integral of a conservative field is exactly 0.

stem: The vector field \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = \left( ([[a]])y + ([[b]])z,\; ([[a]])x + ([[c]])z,\; ([[b]])x + ([[c]])y \right) \) is conservative in \( \mathbb{R}^{3} \). Let \( C \) be a helical closed loop that starts and ends at the same point after two full turns. Evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
solution: A potential is \( \varphi(x,y,z) = ([[a]])xy + ([[b]])xz + ([[c]])yz \), since \( \nabla\varphi = \mathbf{F} \). For a conservative field, the work done around ANY closed loop (in the plane or in space, no matter how many times it winds) is 0 because the path returns to its starting potential value, so \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = 0 \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the three coefficients as if they gave a nonzero circulation per loop; a conservative field's closed-loop integral is always 0.
This doubles the (spurious) per-loop value for the two turns; the correct value for a conservative field is 0 regardless of how many times the loop winds.
This multiplies the coefficients instead of recognizing the closed-loop integral of a gradient field is exactly 0.
This is a small nonzero value; the correct closed-loop integral of a conservative field is exactly 0.
This is a small nonzero value; the correct closed-loop integral of a conservative field is exactly 0.
